rationale: Chemotherapy (CHOP protocol) is first-line standard of care for canine and feline lymphoma per WSAVA Oncology Guidelines
Chemotherapy (vinblastine/prednisolone) is indicated for high-grade or metastatic mast cell tumors per WSAVA guidelines
Adjuvant carboplatin or doxorubicin chemotherapy is standard of care following amputation for osteosarcoma
Splenectomy is the standard of care for splenic masses in dogs regardless of histology — required for both benign and malignant presentations
Emergency splenectomy is indicated for splenic rupture causing hemoabdomen
Limb amputation is the primary surgical treatment for appendicular osteosarcoma — provides best pain control and survival when combined with chemotherapy
Amputation is indicated when limb is non-salvageable due to severe traumatic injury
Radioiodine (I-131) is the gold standard curative treatment for feline hyperthyroidism per AAFP guidelines
Total hip replacement is the definitive surgical treatment for severe hip dysplasia with osteoarthritis in dogs over 20kg
TPLO is the evidence-based surgical standard for cranial cruciate ligament disease in dogs — supported by multiple randomized controlled trials
Permanent cardiac pacing is the only effective long-term treatment for complete heart block in dogs
Renal transplantation is a recognized treatment option for end-stage CKD in cats at ACVS-certified centers
Insulin administration is the cornerstone of treatment for canine and feline diabetes mellitus
AAHA guidelines recommend puppy core vaccine series at 3-4 week intervals — multiple vaccines in one visit is standard protocol
AAFP guidelines recommend kitten core vaccine series — multiple vaccines per visit is standard protocol
Enucleation is indicated for blind painful eyes due to end-stage glaucoma — both prevents pain and eliminates ongoing treatment burden
Enucleation is the treatment of choice for intraocular tumors that are not amenable to other therapy
Subtotal pericardiectomy is indicated for recurrent idiopathic or neoplastic pericardial effusion causing cardiac tamponade
Esophagostomy or gastrostomy tube placement is the standard of care for feline hepatic lipidosis to ensure adequate caloric intake
Assisted feeding via tube is indicated when voluntary food intake is insufficient to meet caloric needs in critically ill patients
